title: Why Your Nervous System Doesn't Trust Good Things
researcherTopic: observation without the observer, freedom from conditioning
metaDescription: Why does your nervous system distrust good things? Learn how trauma wires you to expect the worst and how you can gently guide your body back to a felt sense of safety.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene is quiet and still, evoking a sense of gentle restoration and the beauty of a healed past. The warmth of the light suggests hope and the dawn of a new sense of safety.
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people who describe the same bewildering phenomenon - a life finally filled with good things, a loving partner, a stable job, a quiet home, that nonetheless produces a real sense of unease, a deep and unshakable suspicion. It’s the experience of waiting for the other shoe to drop, even when there is no other shoe, a physiological insistence that safety is a trick and that catastrophe is always just around the corner. This isn't a failure of gratitude or a flaw in your character; it is the entirely predictable echo of a nervous system shaped by a past that refused to be safe, a body still bracing for an impact that is no longer coming.

The Architecture of Expectant Threat

To understand why a nervous system might reject the very peace it has longed for, we have to look at its fundamental architecture. Its primary job is not to make you happy; its primary job is to keep you alive. For a system that developed in an environment of chronic threat, unpredictability, or danger, survival meant becoming exquisitely attuned to the faintest signals of what might go wrong. Good things, in this calculus, are not blessings; they are anomalies that signal an impending return to a chaotic norm. Your brain, in its relentless effort to protect you, became a master of pattern recognition, and the pattern it learned was that connection is followed by abandonment, that quiet is a prelude to rupture, and that love is inextricably linked with pain.

This internal alarm system, once so necessary for survival, doesn't simply turn off when circumstances change. It has been grooved into your being, etched into the neural pathways that govern perception and response. It operates beneath the level of conscious thought, a constant, subliminal scan for danger that colors your entire experience of the world. A client once described this to me as living with a ghost that was always whispering about a future fire while standing in a house that had never been safer. We can learn to recognize this pattern, not as a prophecy, but as a memory - a deeply efficient survival strategy from a different time. This recognition doesn't erase the feeling, but it does contextualize it, stripping it of its immediacy and its power to define the present moment. It is the beginning of a long conversation with a part of ourselves that has worked tirelessly to keep us safe, even when its methods are now causing us pain. For a deeper dive into how early experiences shape our neural pathways, one might explore the concept of the neurobiology of belonging.

The Body's Logic, The Mind's Confusion

The thinking mind struggles to comprehend this paradox. It looks at the evidence of a safe life and concludes that any feeling of dread is irrational, a cognitive error to be corrected. But this is a fundamental misreading of the situation. The philosopher and teacher Jiddu Krishnamurti spoke often of the challenge of observation without the observer, of seeing what is without the filter of our conditioning. When we apply this to our internal world, we see that the body is not being illogical; it is simply running an old, deeply embedded program. It is responding to the felt sense of the past, not the intellectual reality of the present.

When Rest Feels Like a Risk

We often misunderstand this internal resistance, labeling it as self-sabotage or an inability to let go. But it is more accurately understood as a real act of loyalty by a part of you that is still living in the past. It is a testament to how intelligently your system adapted to an untenable situation. The work is not to force this part into submission but to slowly, patiently, demonstrate that the war is over. It might be thirty seconds of noticing the feeling of your feet on the floor, a single conscious breath, or the simple act of feeling the warmth of a cup of tea in your hands. These are not small things; they are the building blocks of a new neural reality, each one a quiet vote for a different future. It is the slow, painstaking work of convincing a sentinel that the war has ended, not with grand pronouncements, but with the irrefutable evidence of one safe moment after another. Understanding this dynamic is central to the work of rewiring the hypervigilant mind.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

I know, I know.

The Upper Limit Problem in a New Light

This reframing is crucial because it moves us out of the territory of self-blame and into the space of compassionate inquiry. Instead of asking, "Why am I ruining things for myself?" we can ask, "What is the capacity of my system for this level of goodness right now?" This question opens up a more skillful path, one that is not about pushing past the limit but about gently expanding it over time. It involves building the container of the body, strengthening the vagal nerve, and increasing the system’s resilience to these higher-frequency states. It is about expanding our window of tolerance not by force, but by gentle and persistent invitation. There is extensive research on the role of the vagus nerve in emotional regulation, much of which can be found in archives like the National Center for Biotechnology Information, which provides a scientific basis for these contemplative practices.

Observation as the Primary Tool

So what is the actual work of learning to trust good things? It is not, as many believe, the work of positive thinking. It is the work of precise, non-judgmental observation. It is about bringing a quiet, steady attention to the mechanics of your own internal world. a moment of connection, a professional success, a simple feeling of peace ~ the practice is to notice the immediate, reflexive response in the body. Notice the tightening in the chest, the subtle clenching of the gut, the thought that says, "This won't last." Notice it all without needing to change it, fix it, or analyze it. It turns out to be the practice of what Krishnamurti called "observation without evaluation." It is the simple, yet striking, act of seeing the mechanism as it is.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

This practice is shifts everything because it introduces a new element into the old equation: a witnessing presence. The old pattern ~ good thing happens, body clenches, mind predicts disaster ~ is an automatic, unconscious loop. By bringing awareness to it, you are interrupting the loop. You are not stopping it, but you are creating a space around it. In that space, something new becomes possible. The body’s clenching is no longer an irrefutable truth; it is a sensation being observed. Over time, this practice of observation begins to metabolize the old fear. The nervous system begins to learn, through direct experience, that it can feel the echo of the old danger and still be safe. It learns that the goodness of the present moment does not have to be extinguished by the memory of the past. What we find here is the core of what it means to heal from the ground up, a topic we explore in making the body a resource, not a liability. It is a movement from reaction to observation, from being fused with our history to being able to hold it with a spacious and compassionate awareness. This shift is everything.

The Slow Thaw of Embodied Safety

Building trust in goodness is not an event; it is a process, a slow thaw. It is the gradual, often imperceptible, accumulation of moments where the present reality is allowed to be more powerful than the past conditioning. It requires a radical kind of patience, a willingness to honor the body's timeline, which is almost always slower than the mind's desire for immediate relief. You can find more of his work on the nature of consciousness and healing. The process is one of titration, of introducing small, manageable doses of safety, connection, and joy, and then pausing to let the system integrate the experience. It is about celebrating the microscopic moments of trust ~ the second you didn’t brace for impact, the moment you allowed a compliment to land, the instant you noticed beauty without the immediate counterpoint of dread.

The question is not whether you are ready. It is whether you are willing. Those are different things, and the difference matters more than you think.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my anxiety spike when good things happen?

It turns out to be a common experience for those with a history of trauma. Your nervous system became wired to associate good feelings with a subsequent threat or loss. When things go well, the old pattern activates, anticipating danger and flooding your system with stress hormones as a protective, albeit outdated, survival mechanism.

For brain health and mood support, a well-regarded option is Nordic Naturals Ultimate Omega (paid link).

Not what you expected, right?

How can I feel safe in my body when it feels like the source of the problem?

The key is to start with micro-doses of presence and connection. Instead of trying to force a feeling of safety, practice simply noticing a neutral sensation, like the feeling of your feet on the ground or the texture of your clothing. This builds the capacity for embodiment slowly, without overwhelming the system's protective responses.

Is it possible to ever fully trust happiness?

Trust is not an absolute state but an ongoing practice of returning. The goal is not to eliminate all fear but to develop the resilience to experience joy and goodness even when the echoes of past unsafety arise. It's about expanding your capacity to hold both the beauty of the present and the memory of the past without the latter hijacking the former.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Nature Made Magnesium Glycinate 400mg (paid link).

Ultimately, the question is not whether you can learn to trust the good things in your life. The question is whether you are willing to undertake the slow, patient, and deeply rewarding work of becoming a safe enough container for them to land.
